title: The Function of Criticism: From The Spectator to Post-Structuralism
excerpt: A genealogical study of literary criticism as a social institution, tracing its transformation from the eighteenth-century public sphere to the specialized academic discipline of the late twentieth century.
keyIdeas: Criticism was born in the coffee houses and periodicals of the eighteenth-century bourgeois public sphere
The decline of criticism is linked to the fragmentation of the public sphere into specialized academic enclaves
Modern criticism has abandoned its political function for sterile professionalism
The rise and fall of criticism mirrors the rise and fall of the bourgeois public sphere
Post-structuralism is the terminal symptom of criticism's loss of social function
keyTakeaways: Criticism was originally a form of political discourse, not academic specialization
The institutionalization of criticism in universities depoliticized it
The decline of the public intellectual is connected to the professionalization of criticism
Recovering criticism's political function requires transforming the social conditions of intellectual work
whoShouldRead: Anyone who worries about the state of public intellectual life
Graduate students in literary studies questioning the purpose of their discipline
Historians of literary criticism and intellectual history
whoShouldSkip: Those seeking practical advice on how to write criticism
Readers who believe academic criticism should be apolitical
